This had been his possession, his might and strength, his steady staff, in the busy, toilsome years of his youth he had learned these three arts, nothing else. He had given her up for what was most miserable, for what was most perishable, for sensual pleasure, for a good life, for riches! It had indeed been strange for him. translation_a: But it turned out that you are just a dignitary, ignorant and stupid, like all the dignitaries who were before you and will be after you. And, like all dignitaries, you are a tyrant. translation_a: As a soccer player Volodya represented a complete opposite to Getzke. Volodya was a professional sportsman芒聙聯the other was a professional player. Important to Volodya was the general course of the game, the overall victory, the outcome芒聙聯Getzke strove only to show his own skill. He was an old, experienced player, not intending to maintain the honor of the team: he valued only his own success; he was not a permanent member of any sort of sports organization because he had compromised himself by moves from club to club for money. They forbade him to participate in matches for the play-off of a championship. They invited him only for friendly games, demonstration matches and trips to other countries. Skill united in him with luck. His participation made the team dangerous. He was suspicious of the players芒聙聯both those with whom he played and the opponents. He knew that he would drive in the balls on any team. The rest was not important to him. He was a hack.
translation_b: As a football player, Volodya was the exact opposite of Getska. Volodya was a professional athlete - he was a professional player. Volodya was interested in the overall course of the game, the overall victory, the outcome - Getsuke sought only to show his art. He was an old, experienced player who did not intend to uphold the honor of the team; he valued only his own success; he was not a permanent member of any sports organization, because he compromised himself by moving from club to club for money. He was banned from participating in championship matches. He was invited only to a friendly game, exhibition matches and trips to other countries. Art was combined in him with luck. His participation made the team dangerous. He despised the players - and those with whom he played, and opponents. He knew that he would score goals for any team. The rest didn't matter to him. He was a hack.

It should be "Getzke" while the author of the second translation writes "Getska" or even "Getsuke". 2) According to the source text a story is told about two characters. The second sentence of the passage should give information about two different people while there is only one person mentioned in the second sentence of the 2nd translation: "Volodya was a professional athlete - he was a professional player" 3) The author of the second translation uses a word-by-word translation of the sentence "He despised the players - and those with whom he played, and opponents" which cannot be used here

translation_a: Since he often jumped from one thing to another and forgot what he had begun to say, the prince kept still so as to let him speak everything out. But all the same it was extremely unclear whether the letters had gone through him or through Vera. If he himself insisted that 芒聙聹to Rogozhin was all the same as to Nastasya Filippovna,芒聙?it meant that most likely they had not gone through him, if there were any letters at all. And how it happened that the letter now ended up with him, remained decidedly unexplained; most likely of all would be to suppose that he had somehow stolen it from Vera 芒聙?carried it off on the sly and taken it with some sort of intention to Lizaveta Prokofyevna. So the prince finally figured it out and understood it.
translation_b: Since he often strayed from one to the other and forgot what he began to talk about, the prince fell silent to let him speak. But all the same, it was extremely unclear: did the letters go through him or through Vera? If he himself assured that "to Rogozhin is the same as to Nastasya Filippovna," then it means, rather, that they did not go through him, if only there were letters. The incident, how the letter now came to him, remained decidedly unexplained; most likely it was necessary to assume that he somehow stole it from Vera ... quietly stole it and took it with some intention to Lizaveta Prokofievna. So the prince thought and understood at last.
human_comment: The first translation is a better one for various reasons: Grammar: according to the source text Past perfect should be used to identify a series of actions that happened before other actions in the past. Past Perfect is used in the first translation Vocabulary: "And how it happened that the letter now ended up with him" in the first translation is more accurate and conveys the meaning better than "The incident, how the letter now came to him" in the second translation. The same thing with "carried it off on the sly" (1st translation) over "quietly stole it". Moreover, there is a tautology in the second translation: "he somehow stole it from Vera ... quietly stole it and took it". The last sentence in the second translation isn't accurate and doesn't sum up the idea presented in the passage, which makes the sentence in the first translation better.
deepseek_comment: Translation B more accurately captures the original's nuanced syntax and tone, particularly in rendering the conditional logic and the phrase '褌懈褏芯薪褜泻芯 褍泻褉邪谢' as 'quietly stole it,' which better preserves the narrative voice.
gemini_comment: Translation A flows more naturally in English and handles the punctuation and sentence structure more effectively. Translation B's use of "If he himself assured that" is unidiomatic, whereas A's "insisted that" or "asserted" would be better (though A uses "insisted").

translation_a: The Christian teaching given to me in childhood disappeared in me as in others, only with the difference that as I began to read a great deal and to think very early on, so my rejection of Christian teaching became a conscious one very early on. From the age of sixteen I stopped saying my prayers and of my own volition stopped going to church and fasting. I stopped believing in all that had been given to me since childhood, but I did believe in something. What I did believe in I couldn芒聙聶t possibly have said. I did believe in God or rather did not deny God, but I couldn芒聙聶t have said what kind of god; I didn芒聙聶t deny Christ and his teaching, but I also couldn芒聙聶t have said where his teaching lay.
translation_b: The doctrine communicated to me from childhood disappeared in me just as it did in others, with the only difference being that since I began to read and think a lot very early, my renunciation of the doctrine became conscious very early. From the age of sixteen, I stopped standing up for prayer and stopped, on my own impulse, going to church and fasting. I stopped believing in what I was told from childhood, but I believed in something. What I believed in, I could never say. I also believed in God, or rather I did not deny God, but which God I could not say; I did not deny Christ and his teaching, but what his teaching was, I could not say either.
human_comment: I consider the first translation to be more accurate when it comes to certain details: 1) The source text makes it clear that the narrator tells about Christian teaching, that is why I don't see any reason to call it just "doctrine" as in the second translation 2) The phrase "of my own volition" in the first translation conveys the meaning of the source sentence much better than "on my own impulse" in the second translation 3) According to the source text the narrator "couldn芒聙聶t have said what kind of god" he believed, that is why the translation "which God I could not say" in the second translation is inappropriate 4) If we consider the context of the source text I believe it's better to use Past Perfect in the sentences that refer to the prior actions in the past. translation_a: This unexpected blow nearly killed my father. He lost his usual firmness, and his grief (usually mute) poured out in bitter complaints. "How!" he repeated, losing his temper. - 芒聙聹My son participated in the plans of Pugachev! Good God, what have I come to! The empress saves him from execution! Does that make it easier for me? The execution is not terrible: my ancestor died at the place of execution, defending what he considered the shrine of his conscience; my father suffered along with Volynsky and Khrushchev. But the nobleman should change his oath, unite with robbers, murderers, runaway lackeys!.. Shame and disgrace to our family! human opinion. My father was inconsolable.
translation_b: This unexpected blow very nearly killed my father. He lost his habitual self-control, and his grief, usually silent, found expression in bitter complaints. 芒聙聹What!芒聙?he repeated, beside himself. 芒聙聹My son is an accomplice of Pugachov芒聙聶s! Merciful heavens, what have I lived to see! The Empress reprieves him! Does that make it any better for me? It芒聙聶s not the death penalty that is terrible. My great-grandfather died on the scaffold for what was to him a matter of conscience; my father suffered, together with Volynsky and Khrushchov.a But for a gentleman to betray his oath of allegiance and join brigands, murderers and runaway serfs! Shame and disgrace to our name!芒聙?Terrified by his despair, my mother did not dare to weep in his presence and tried to cheer him by talking of the uncertainty of rumor and the small faith to be attached to people芒聙聶s opinions. translation_a: The news of my arrest was a shock to my family. Marya Ivanovna had told my parents of my strange acquaintance with Pugachov so simply that, so far from being troubled about it, they often laughed at it with whole-hearted amusement. My father refused to believe that I could have been implicated in vile rebellion the aim of which was to overthrow the throne and exterminate the gentry. He closely questioned Savelyich. The old man did not conceal the fact that I had been to see Pugachov and that the villain had been kind to me; but he swore that he had not heard of any treason. My parents were reassured and waited impatiently for favorable news. Marya Ivanovna was very much alarmed but said nothing, for she was extremely modest and prudent.
translation_b: The news of my arrest shocked my entire family. Marya Ivanovna told my parents so simply about my strange acquaintance with Pugachev that it not only did not disturb them, but also made them often laugh from the bottom of their hearts. Batiushka did not want to believe that I could be involved in a vile rebellion, the purpose of which was the overthrow of the throne and the extermination of the noble family. He severely interrogated Savelich. The uncle did not hide the fact that the master was visiting Emelka Pugachev, and that the villain still complained about something; but he swore that he had never heard of any betrayal. The old people calmed down and began to look forward to favorable news. Marya Ivanovna was greatly alarmed, but kept silent, for she was gifted to the highest degree with modesty and caution.
human_comment: The first translation is more accurate. Let's have a look at the grammar. The source text makes it clear that Past Perfect should be used to identify the sequence of actions in the past: "The old man did not conceal the fact that I had been to see Pugachov and that the villain had been kind to me" but he swore that he had not heard of any treason" and it is used in the first translation. Moreover, the sentence mentioned above is incorrectly translated in the second translation, which makes it impossible to understand the whole passage. Vocabulary. The second translation doesn't provide an accurate translation of several words. It should be "My father" (as in the first translation) but not "Batiushka", "The old man" but not "The uncle", "My parents" but not "The old people "

translation_a: He did not finish; Ferdyshchenko quickly put a chair behind him, and the general, somewhat weak on his feet that afternoon, flopped down, or rather, fell on the chair, but this, however, did not embarrass him. He sat down directly opposite Nastasya Filippovna and with a pleasant grin, slowly and effectively, raised her fingers to his lips. In general, it was rather difficult to embarrass the general. His outward appearance, besides some slovenliness, was still quite decent, which he himself knew very well. He had happened before to be in very good society, from which he was finally excluded only two or three years ago. From the same period, he indulged too much without restraint in some of his weaknesses; but the dexterous and pleasant manner remained in him hitherto. Nastasya Filippovna seemed to be overjoyed at the appearance of Ardalion Alexandrovich, whom, of course, she knew from hearsay.
translation_b: He did not finish. Ferdyshchenko quickly offered him a chair from behind, and the general, somewhat weak in the legs after dinner, simply flopped or, better to say, collapsed into it; however, that did not embarrass him. He sat directly facing Nastasya Filippovna and, with a pleasant little grimace, slowly and dramatically brought her fingers to his lips. On the whole, it was rather difficult to embarrass the general. His appearance, apart from a certain slovenliness, was still quite decent, as he knew very well himself. In the past he had occasionally been received in very good society, from which he had been definitively excluded only two or three years ago. It was then that he gave himself over all too unrestrainedly to some of his weaknesses; but he still retained his adroit and pleasant manner. Nastasya Filippovna, it seemed, was exceedingly delighted by the appearance of Ardalion Alexandrovich, of whom she knew, of course, by hearsay.
human_comment: Both translations are quite good, but I prefer the second translation to the first one. The vocabulary in the second translation is more accurate: "weak in the legs" over "weak on his feet", "brought her fingers to his lips" over "raised her fingers to his lips", "was exceedingly delighted by the appearance of Ardalion Alexandrovich" over "seemed to be overjoyed at the appearance". Such small details lead to the understanding of the whole text.
deepseek_comment: Translation B more effectively captures the nuanced tone and precise physical descriptions, such as 'collapsed into it' for '褍锌邪谢 薪邪 褋褌褍谢' and 'adroit' for '谢芯胁泻邪褟', enhancing both accuracy and literary flow.
gemini_comment: Translation B is more idiomatic and captures the nuances of the Russian text more effectively. For example, 'weak in the legs after dinner' is a better rendering of the post-prandial context than 'weak on his feet that afternoon', and 'dramatically' captures the sense of '褝褎褎械泻褌薪芯' better than 'effectively'.

translation_a: But now I allow myself to say that this was a cruel demand, at the same time I did not even think of it, I was only inexpressibly hurt. I was no longer in the position in which I was in my youth, thinking that everything in life is clear; After all, I came to faith because, besides faith, I found nothing, probably nothing, except death, so it was impossible to cast aside this faith, and I submitted. And I found in my soul a feeling that helped me bear it. It was a feeling of self-humiliation and humility. I resigned myself, swallowed this blood and body without blasphemous feeling, with a desire to believe, but the blow had already been dealt. And, knowing in advance what awaited me, I could no longer go another time.
translation_b: But now I let myself say it was a brutal requirement; then I didn芒聙聶t even think that, it was just inexpressibly painful for me. I was no longer in the situation I had been in my younger days, thinking that everything in life was clear; I had come to faith because apart from faith I had found nothing, really nothing but annihilation, so I couldn芒聙聶t reject this faith and I submitted. And I found a feeling in my soul that helped me to bear it. This was a feeling of self-abasement and humility. I humbled myself; I swallowed this flesh and blood without any feeling of blasphemy, with the desire to believe, but the blow had been struck. And knowing in advance what was waiting for me, I could no longer go a second time.
human_comment: The style of the source text is better conveyed in the second translation. The vocabulary in the second translation is better and more accurate. translation_a: 芒聙聹And what then? I saw gracious, simple-hearted, intelligent people; I saw an old man who was gentle and heard out a boy like me; I see people capable of understanding and forgiveness, people who are Russian and kind, people almost as kind and cordial as I met there, almost no worse. You can judge how joyfully surprised I was! Oh, allow me to speak this out! I had heard a lot and believed very much myself that in society everything is a manner, everything is a decrepit form, while the essence is exhausted; but I can see for myself now that among us that cannot be; anywhere else, but not among us. Can it be that you are all now Jesuits and swindlers? I heard Prince N. tell a story tonight: wasn芒聙聶t it all artless, inspired humor, wasn芒聙聶t it genuinely good-natured? Can such words come from the lips of a 芒聙?dead man, with a dried-up heart and talent? Could dead people have treated me the way you have treated me?
translation_b: - So what? I saw graceful, simple-hearted, intelligent people; I saw an old man who caresses and listens to the boy, like me; I see people who are able to understand and forgive, Russian and kind people, almost as kind and warm-hearted as I met there, almost no worse. Judge how joyfully I was surprised! Oh, let me say it! I heard a lot and I myself very much believed that in the world everything is a manner, everything is a decrepit form, and the essence has dried up; but I myself now see that this cannot be with us; it is somewhere, but not here. Are you all now Jesuits and deceivers? I heard Prince N. say just now: Isn't this simple-hearted, inspired humor, isn't this true good nature? Can such words come out of the mouth of a man... dead, with a withered heart and talent? Could the dead have treated me like you did?
human_comment: The first translation is better. Speaking of grammar Past Perfect should be used as in the first translation: "I had heard a lot and believed very much...". The first translation conveys the meaning properly by using accurate vocabulary: "among us that cannot be; anywhere else, but not among us" over "this cannot be with us; it is somewhere, but not here", "I heard Prince N. tell a story tonight" is way better than "I heard Prince N. say just now", "lips" should be used as a better equivalent to the Russian word, but not "mouth" (the second translation)
